column1: What are you responsible for when using AWS Fargate?
column2: Defining task definitions and services that specify container images and resource requirements (not managing EC2 instances)

column1: What does the minimumHealthyPercent parameter specify in ECS?
column2: The minimum percentage of tasks that must remain in Running state during deployment to maintain service availability

column1: How does ECS handle tasks when there are insufficient resources during a service update?
column2: ECS continues to attempt to launch tasks until successful; increase cluster size to add resources or set minimum in-service percentage to 0% to allow termination before launch

column1: Who manages the underlying cluster instances when using Fargate?
column2: AWS provisions and manages underlying cluster instances; you don't need to manage maintenance and patching

column1: What is the Amazon ECS agent responsible for?
column2: All on-instance tasks including downloading container images and starting or stopping containers

column1: What is service-oriented architecture (SOA) in the context of containers?
column2: Using containers to implement discrete application components separately from one another to ensure availability and durability of each component

column1: How many containers can a single ECS task definition describe?
column2: Up to 10 containers; to launch more, create multiple task definitions grouped by similar purpose, lifecycle, or resource requirements

column1: What is the most secure way to store and access sensitive data in Amazon ECS?
column2: Use AWS Secrets Manager to store secrets and inject them as environment variables at runtime (not in task definitions or container images)

column1: What ECS feature enables launching multiple copies of the same container on different ports?
column2: Dynamic host port mapping with Application Load Balancer (not supported by Classic Load Balancer)

column1: What ECS task placement policy distributes tasks across multiple availability zones and cluster instances?
column2: Spread policy (considers availability zones when placing tasks)

column1: What is the best approach for storing many related configuration parameters and sensitive values in ECS?
column2: Use Parameter Store for hierarchical configuration parameters and Secrets Manager for sensitive values

column1: What network mode must Fargate use?
column2: awsvpc network mode (provides each task with its own ENI and private IP address within VPC; bridge and host modes only available on EC2 clusters)

column1: What is the advantage of using Secrets Manager with RDS for credential rotation?
column2: Automatic credential rotation without direct involvement from administrators; applications simply fetch current values

column1: What is responsible for assigning unique IP addresses to pods in EKS clusters?
column2: Amazon VPC Container Network Interface (CNI) plug-in (assigns IPs from VPC address space to each pod)

column1: What EKS feature helps scale cluster capacity by adding new nodes via Auto Scaling?
column2: Cluster Autoscaler (adds nodes when pods cannot be scheduled due to resource constraints)

column1: What does the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler do in EKS?
column2: Scales the number of pods up and down based on metrics (does not add cluster resources or nodes)

column1: Can you migrate an EKS cluster from EC2 to Fargate?
column2: Yes, it can be done incrementally to take advantage of Fargate's serverless container management

column1: What is the purpose of ECS services?
column2: Designed to always maintain desired number of tasks and start new ones if tasks fail

column1: Does changing ECS cluster capacity affect service scaling configuration?
column2: No, changing cluster capacity does not affect service scaling settings

column1: Should you hard-code secrets in container images even if encrypted?
column2: No, never hard-code secrets in images due to risk of leaking to version control and requiring full rebuild for updates
